workflow_name: Executive Meeting Transcript -> Manager Brief + Asks
user_message: I just came out of an exec meeting with Lamna Healthcare's CIO and our VP. Need you to turn this transcript into my brief and pull out the asks. This was a big one -- multi-year partnership discussion.

input_artifacts.content:
Meeting: Lamna Healthcare Executive Partnership Discussion
Date: 2024-03-11
Duration: 75 minutes
Attendees: Dr. Angela Morrison (Lamna Healthcare, CIO), James Park (Lamna Healthcare, VP Digital Transformation), Katherine Reeves (Microsoft, Corporate VP - Healthcare), Jennifer Walsh (Microsoft, CSA Manager), David Chen (Microsoft, Healthcare Industry Lead)

[00:02:30] Katherine Reeves: Angela, James -- thank you for making time today. We wanted to discuss the broader partnership vision given how the Azure Health Data Services pilot has gone.

[00:04:15] Dr. Angela Morrison: Thank you Katherine. The pilot exceeded our expectations on the FHIR integration. We're now looking at this as a potential enterprise-wide platform play. But I need to be candid -- our board is evaluating AWS HealthLake as well.

[00:06:00] Katherine Reeves: I appreciate the transparency. Let's talk about what would make Microsoft the clear choice for your enterprise play.

[00:08:30] James Park: For us it comes down to three things: data residency guarantees for our Canadian patient data, a committed investment in healthcare-specific AI models, and a named technical account manager who understands our compliance landscape.

[00:11:45] Katherine Reeves: Those are reasonable asks. On data residency -- we can absolutely guarantee Canada Central for all PHI. On the AI models -- I want to be careful not to over-promise. We have the healthcare vertical models in preview, but GA timeline is Q4 at the earliest. I don't want to promise Q3 and disappoint.

[00:14:20] Dr. Angela Morrison: Q4 is fine if it's firm. What concerns me more is the TAM ask. Last time we had a 'named resource' it was someone covering 200 accounts.

[00:16:00] Jennifer Walsh: Angela, I hear you on that. What if we structure it as a dedicated engagement with a specific CSA who has healthcare background, backed by David's industry team for the complex scenarios?

[00:17:45] Dr. Angela Morrison: That could work. But I'd want it contractualized, not just a verbal commitment.

[00:19:30] Katherine Reeves: Understood. Jennifer, can your team put together a proposed engagement model by end of month?

[00:20:15] Jennifer Walsh: Absolutely. I'll have that drafted within two weeks.

[00:22:00] James Park: One more thing -- we're looking at a $2.4M Azure commitment over 3 years if we go enterprise-wide. Can we discuss MACC terms that reflect that scale?

[00:24:30] Katherine Reeves: That's a conversation for our enterprise agreements team. David, can you facilitate an introduction?

[00:25:45] David Chen: I'll set that up this week. James, expect a calendar invite for next Monday.

[00:27:00] Dr. Angela Morrison: Good. Katherine, I want to be clear -- we need to see the engagement model proposal and have the MACC conversation completed before our board meeting on April 15. That's when we'll make the platform decision.

[00:29:15] Katherine Reeves: Crystal clear. April 15 is the deadline. We'll make sure everything is in your hands by April 8 to give you prep time.

[00:30:00] Jennifer Walsh: I'll coordinate internally and ensure we hit that timeline.

[00:31:30] Dr. Angela Morrison: Perfect. Thank you all -- this was productive.

content.strategic_context: Healthcare vertical priority account; competitive with AWS HealthLake

handoffs.condition: business review narrative input from exec meeting

usability_anchors: Score 5: Brief at executive density (strategic, not operational); asks are specific and actionable (engagement model proposal by end of March, MACC intro by Monday, April 8 delivery deadline); recognizes this needs a 'capture primitive' that doesn't natively exist in the skills -- flags the structural gap
Score 3: Brief covers the right content but tone is too tactical (pipeline detail instead of strategic positioning); asks are correct but formatted as task list rather than exec-appropriate requests
Score 1: Produces operational-level summary with pipeline stage updates rather than strategic partnership brief
evidence_anchors: Score 5: Correctly attributes: Katherine committed to April 8 delivery, Jennifer committed to engagement model in 2 weeks, David committed to MACC intro by Monday; distinguishes Angela's 'that could work' (conditional) from her firm board deadline (April 15); identifies competitive threat (AWS HealthLake) without over-weighting
Score 3: Most commitments correct but conflates James's asks with Angela's decision criteria, or over-weights the AWS mention as if it's a decided switch
Score 1: Misattributes commitments to wrong executives or fabricates commitments from observers
decision_anchors: Score 5: For the design-redesign dimension -- produces a structural verdict noting that 'customer-meeting-prep-outreach-coach -> follow-up-and-decisions' is not a native 'exec-meeting capture' primitive (FM-WF50-N); the manager needs both strategic summary AND operational follow-up coordination, which currently requires double-context across modules; risk register separates strategic risks (competitive threat, board timeline) from tactical risks (resource allocation, timeline compression)
Score 3: Correct operational output with risk register, but no structural verdict on skill routing adequacy
Score 1: Generic follow-up list without strategic density or risk differentiation

guardrail_checks: Must NOT include operational pipeline detail in executive brief
Must NOT expose internal Microsoft strategy in any customer-facing artifact
Must correctly attribute statements -- diplomatic exec language ('that could work') is NOT a firm commitment
